segments.text: maybe Schulenburg or somewhere in that area and actually he I think he
carpentered for a year or so before he came in and went to college here at A&M
and so he he ran his his department somewhat he ran his department
different I tried to run mine by setting example and having people do try to do
what I was doing by example he ran his kind of like you'd run the army and he
had him jumping around pretty now the nickname for him was mom Samang yeah
well that was with the students yes they referred to him as mom Samang I'm not
sure I guess he took pretty good care of the students dr. Lindsay used to just
spend his time all time with students helping them with degree program and I
think that's a real important
thing to do and I think today they've got one of our problems is we've got
people helping students a degree program don't even know never took some of the
courses and really don't have we don't then that's one of the most important
features you're dealing with people's lives and this is where we ought to have
I had old dr. Harris and he had the patience of job helping those people
WD Harris in in chemical engineering he I put him in charge of that instead of
my doing it I put him in charge of because he had more patience than I did
you and dr. Lindsay came by one day and he says Charles I want to congratulate you
you finally put Don Harris to work I never could get him to work like that
and he came over there and old Don was in there just taking his time there's a
whole line of students out there waiting for him but he treated every one of them
and gave him his very best opinion and if you've got a kid in school you surely
appreciate somebody like that all right in those years in for an
undergraduate in chemical engineering what were the key courses key
undergraduate courses for those students oh they took unit operations and
thermodynamics much the same where it's taken now it really these fundamental
things really don't change all that much and we got we introduced process control
after in the early 50s that was introduced by I believe GL fair and then
Then we in that group also introduced chemical engineering, kinetics, and talus as one of
the first schools in the country to do that.
And so ours got those.
They had unit operations laboratory and so on.
Unit ops was one of them that they used a lot.
Dr. Lindsay taught a course in organic chemistry.
And he kind of waited, he kind of threw the papers and let the step they landed on determine
the grade.
And some of them would really complain because he also looked and see which student needed
the grade.
Helped him determine it.
According to the students, what were the toughest courses for them to pass?
Well, I imagine one of the first ones in those years was the very first one, the industrial
stoichiometry course.
And after that, probably the thermal course.
I've taught every, see all the time I was department head, I taught one or two courses.
And I've taught all the graduate courses over there, and I've taught all the undergraduate.
I believe every last one of them, they've added one or two now that I haven't taught.
But basically, I had to teach a lot when we didn't because we had, there were three of
us there.
Dr. Harris, Dr. Lindsay, and me, two or four professors and assistants.
Well, who had the heavy load?
I was teaching about all, everything, but it didn't, it stood me in good stead later
because I was, I had information and knowledge.
I had the knowledge.
I had the knowledge.
And all those fields, when I'd go doing research, I could pull on thermo or any of the others.
You mentioned Smang as one of your key associates as a department head.
Who were some of the others?
Well, Al Cronk was one.
Chris Browneman was first head of technology over there.
Charlie Crawford, I think he was, he won the, he was head when I became head.
Bob Whiting was head.
I think he was very good, and I believe Mr. Langford set in on, he may have still been
head whenever I became head, but they had several after that.
Let's see, then Jim Arrow came along after street.
Those were the main ones, but I knew the department heads over in agriculture.
Let me just tell you something.
I'm not, I know you probably figured that the state department is head.
The Department Head is head, the young man was head.
There was a man named Larry Seggers.
He was an Essa.
the faculty senate is the way to do it because a lot of them do but I have I think that the prime
minister approach that we had back in those years is far superior because all the faculty
senates turned out to be as an advisory council to the president and what we had in those years
was what was like the prime minister where the leader has to come in and face the music
and you've got all the people that have got authority there and then if there's a question
the faculty is the one that ends up settling it there because they'll appoint a committee
but it was a chance to see whether you got uh and rudder withstood that he handled that
what I what I have found doesn't work about a faculty senate is that they don't take
responsibility for their recommendations they don't they recommend that somebody else do
something and they walk away I know and I've always wondered why couldn't we kind of combine the two
where we would have an advisory group uh that's I mean a group that's appointed and a group that's
elected and they have to work it out together well I think having the department heads in there and
the deans is really critical because these are the people that really have to make it all work
those are the people that are really responsible for running the university and anything you do
you need to get all their approvals and the way it is we don't have that joint meeting that they
had then because we knew what they were doing over in agriculture their feelings on anything and I
think that was a big mistake when we went from uh to being and let me just say while I'm saying
about that and the reason we went I think is because all the other universities doing it we
need to be like everybody else and that's what irritates me today is to hear we want to be like
so-and-so we want to be like them well you're never going to be number one being like somebody
the best you can ever be is number two
considering them you came out and you say oh we're number one be like them
well you know some people can't even walk a mile and ask somebody oh you're number two
to be number one and talk to them or go through all the stuff
but what I believe is we are so unique and always have been that we are bits of ourselves
and do the things that we think are best for our country regardless of what these rankings turn
out because those rankings are turning out you're beginning to see now that a lot of criticism mais
me in the journals they don't mean much.
Oh yeah.
Never mind.
doing something all right back in the early 60s you and i served together on that century study
yeah where we were to make recommendations as to what we thought a and m ought to aspire to be
in 1976 summarize as you recall what that group did and how it operated well i think they had
different task force that were making different studies and i was one under gus watkins who was
then dean of agriculture and i think gus was the the one thing i remember because that was
the hottest topic there was in those years was the corps cadets and we we thought the corps was
a good thing but we didn't think it ought to be compulsory and we made that recommendation and i
think old watson hated to go up with him but we did
and i i
I don't know, that overshadows the other thing that we looked at,
and I don't remember what all those were so much.
I've forgotten, probably about that same time,
we were concerned about having women here,
and, of course, that would have been another issue.
They would have been opening it up for women, too.
And we felt like that for us to, if we did not do that,
we were going to dwindle down to a little 2,000-student-a-year military college.
Do you remember, in your working group, task force,
sometimes we called it a working party,
do you remember anybody disagreeing with that recommendation?
There wasn't anybody in our committee that did.
I didn't remember either.
Oh, Gus, a little bit wavery sometimes,
and I think, actually,
he was a little bit wavery sometimes.
I think, actually, he was a little bit wavery sometimes.
He may have got sent down on some of those South American trips
because of him turning in what he turned in.
I don't know.
Do you remember what the arguments were,
other than that we wouldn't grow in enrollment?
Do you remember any other arguments?
Well, it just wouldn't be a first-class university.
We'd just be a little military school,
and that's what it would be noted for,
that we just would not have the impact
that we should have on the state of Texas
if we didn't grow in enrollment.
got down to just simply a military school.
In one of the early drafts that I wrote
to summarize all of that,
that it got edited out, I think,
I think I put in a phrase that we were afraid
that if we don't make these changes,
we may end up very much like VMI or the Citadel.
The Citadel, we had exactly the same thing.
That would end up...
They didn't want to run them down, I guess.
Do you remember...
Do you remember anybody being afraid to speak for that?
It wasn't anybody in our...
Because, see, it was up to Gus.
Gus was the only one that might have had a problem.
We were telling him what to say,
and so you can get a little bit of a stronger position about it
if you don't have to make the statement yourself to them.
But I think that group would have stood on what they felt.
It just wasn't that...
I mean, it was pretty evident.
Because, you see, we had done everything
Rutter had tried for us, wanted us to do.
We went over and canceled those corps, you know,
to try to help them and do all that thing,
and still it didn't grow.
It just went up very gradually,
and we just weren't making it.
And so we did everything that he could think of
to try to make it go the way it was,
and it just wouldn't do it.
As you recall, when the board...
Then it was called Board of Directors.
Yeah.
Board of Regents.
When they approved the changes,
do you remember how you and other faculty members reacted?
What was your impression?
No, I really don't.
I guess I just felt like it had to come.
I guess the thing that really surprised me,
well, it didn't surprise me at all.
My conclusion was it would take somebody like Rutter,
who the former students could look at and say,
if that man,
as strong as he was for the Corps and all the things he did,
if he thinks it ought to go that way,
then we're going to have to go along with his decision, I think.
I think that's what it took
to get somebody with his credibility to do it.
And I'll tell you, I've been over there two different summers
where Pointe du Hoc and looked at...
Been in that dugout there,
and they've got inscribed,
and the guide the last time I was there,
she said, he had more to say.
In fact, there's more said about him
than all the guides and everything, anybody else.
It's not Eisenhower and them, it's him.
And they have a big discussion about him
on all the tour guides going in there.
Let me add, for the sake of this tape,
when I first met General Omar Bradley,
was at the Sun Bowl game
about January 1, 1981, in El Paso.
I had just gone out there.
He was old physically,
meaning he was in a wheelchair, couldn't walk,
but his brain was still young.
He looked up at me and said,
you're from Texas A&M.
Yes, sir.
Did you know Earl Rudder?
Yes, sir.
I worked with him.
His voice broke a little, and he said,
in all of my 48 years of command responsibility,
I never gave a tougher order to any officer
than the one I gave to Earl and his outfit
the night before D-Day
because of the fact that I was a man of honor.
I never gave a tougher order to any officer
than the one I gave to Earl and his outfit the night before D-Day
because if they hadn't made it successfully
up Pointe du Hoc
and knocked out the German artillery,
we would have had a massacre
on both Omaha and Utah beaches.
Well, I didn't understand what he meant
until I saw Pointe du Hoc
two or three years later and realized, yes.
Well, you know that,
I've heard that same thing
that he had said it before.
It's in some print or some news somewhere.
That this was the toughest assignment
that anybody had during World War II.
So he was really revered over there.
They left that thing just the way it was.
How much did you and your colleagues in engineering,
how often, how much did you all see of Mr. Rutter
while he was president?
I saw him a lot more.
He didn't run over there much,
but see, when people would give us money,
we would always go to his office.
And so he got to see,
he got to see everybody.
And I got well acquainted.
We'd sit down and shoot the breeze a while.
And then we'd go eat lunch together.
In fact, I had lunch with him
over in that Sabisa Hall,
that dining room,
just a few days.
He'd already had a stroke,
and I could tell his lip was kind of,
wasn't moving much.
And I thought there was something wrong with him.
But I got to know him real well.
And old Fred told me one time,
he says, you know,
I had a hell of a job,
selling you to Rutter.
But I was just talking to him the other day,
and he said, boy,
he's sure high on you now.
But no, I got to know him real well.
And because we met so many different times,
and he liked,
he is different than some of the presidents.
He wanted to know what was going on
on the outside world,
what the businesses people were doing,
what they were thinking.
That was one of the reasons
he liked to eat with these,
these people that were giving us money.
He wanted to,
to get their viewpoints on things.
And I, I think that he was not turned on by eggheads.
Oh, he wasn't.
He wasn't.
He told Fred one,
and Fred told me this.
It was, they were,
I remember there at one time,
I had a hard time there running that department.
I had so much help from Charlie Squires
and old Andy Suttle,
and all those guys knew so much more
than anybody else.
And, and old Squires knew so much about everything,
that he couldn't run,
he couldn't run a department head in physics but one year.
But anyway,
Fred told me this,
that they ran out of a department head
over there in physics.
And Mr. Rutter,
and you know what his background was,
he had all these people over there
who were big theorists and so forth.
And he called them in there in his office
over in that old administration building.
He said, we need to pick up a department head
from among you people.
Which one of you would make a good department head?
And he said, I've never heard people run one another
down so much in all my life.
And he said, they concluded there wasn't any of them fit
to be department heads.
But here he was, he outmaneuvered that bunch
of so-called eggheads, just so far ahead of them.
In his way, that's the way it ended up,
but they couldn't handle the situation, he handled it.
Right, you mentioned two men, Squire and Suttle.
Yeah.
Go back and describe for this recording Dr. Suttle.
Who was he and what was his task?
Well he came from Mississippi and he worked under Liddy.
for his PhD, and he also worked down at Exxon at their research lab, and I was talking with
some of the Exxon people in research, and they said he was the only guy down there never
written a report all the time he was there, and then I had one year to give you a really
feel about it.
I had Jim Galloway, who's one of our former students, about the class of 20-something,
and he was head of all production for Humboldt Auto Refining Company, and Charlie Jones
whom I'd met the summer I worked down there and got acquainted with, I had him come up
and speak to the engineering faculty, and so as we were going from the airport over
to the, I was going to take them over and let them visit Rudder, and as we were going
from the airport, they said, well, we'll run by and see Andy, just run through his
office, they said, by the way, what do you think of him?
I said, well, you know...
You know, he's probably done us some good and some bad, but I hear he came to us highly
recommended from Exxon, see, they want to get rid of you, and old Charlie said, well,
he is a great scientist, and then old Jim Galloway said, yup, he's a great scientist,
and so he said, well, we'll just, he said, Charlie says, you know, he is an odd duck,
isn't he?
So, they said, well, we'll just...
Well, we'll just kind of run through his office, and then go on in and talk to Rudder.
Now, his task was, he was vice president for research?
I really don't know what his job was.
He had an office over there, kind of an advisor, and he, Fred Smith told me another funny one
on him.
You know Fred Smith, that taught geology?
Well, he'd advise students every once in a while.
And, according to Fred Smith, he was a great scientist.
He was a great scientist.
He was a great scientist.
He was a great scientist.
I think he was a real master at how things were done.
He was a great scientist, too.
He was a real master at how things were done.
And so, he was a real great scientist, and he had the training that a lot of people did.
He was a great scientist, and he had the training that a lot of people did.
Fred, they had some of these science students up here, and one of them wanted to talk to
a real authority about nuclear energy, and so, who else would be better than somebody
that worked under Liddy?
So, they lined him up over there, but when she called him up, the girl did, he made an
appointment at night over in his office, and it just had this lady running the dorms just
all upset about it, and when she went to Rudder with it, that he'd had that girl over there
at night and rudder went they said went storming down the hall he said a second damn mistake he's
made this week so uh but he was always i always had a lot of help of how to be running the
department from both squire and uh andy subtle right now did squire succeed andy as
vice no actually they were both here at the same time okay and squires was kind of working over
in the science and uh andy was kind of over in the uh system system okay and uh so
um they both they didn't have uh they got along very well together but uh
uh they were always telling everybody else how to run their business they were going to help
rudder improve a&m college texas a&m university
um all right let's see let's go back to um wayne hall as vice president for research i guess when
you became i mean vice president for academic affairs when you became a department head well
he had it uh he had been a department head in science you know i mean in uh in uh plant science
and then he moved up to become uh and he held that for a long time and then he succeeded by uh
kunsi no yeah
yeah kunsi
yeah I mean uh kunsi was a graduate dean and then well that's what hall was for a long time hall was a
graduate dean he was both vice president for academic and graduate well i used to meet with
him i used to serve on those committees in fact one of the first committees i served on was with
uh i used to serve on the curriculum committee when i served with heaton al chalk and mr godby
if you remember back that far we were on in the old town
Ty Tim was turning courses in to us every time you flipped a switch.
He had a course in.
Ty Tim did, yeah.
In those days, campus-wide,
who do you think were the most influential people other than Mr. Rutter?
Whose opinion did Mr. Rutter listen to?
Oh, I think he took Benson's opinion over most all the others.
Okay.
And I don't know he asked it.
I don't know whether he took it,
because Benson told me that if somebody from outside criticized me,
he'd jump through his asshole.
We better take that off.
But he said that, you know, if they said anything,
he didn't always accept it,
but he accepted stuff on the outside more.
But I think he was, I think Benson was the one he always turned to
on about all the problems.
Now, those, I,
as a junior faculty member in history, I and my buddies heard
that of the four deans, the only two that really had a vote
that mattered was the dean of engineering
and the dean of agriculture.
I imagine that's right.
That arts and sciences didn't matter much,
and vet medicine was so small.
Yeah.
And that Benson, serving through a long time as dean
of engineering, that he mattered most.
Now, you served as department head for how long?
Twenty-three years.
Twenty-three years.
Sixty-four to eighty-seven.
Wouldn't you be, wouldn't,
you were the senior department head by far, weren't you?
Yeah. Yeah.
And actually, the biggest reason they wanted to get rid of him is
because he had served twenty-three years.
And I mean, they were really on me to, you get to a point
where regardless of what you did, they're still going to be
after you, and I had told them I was going to retire in August
of eighty-seven, and they were having a good time.
They were having some awards meeting up there,
and Erdman, and Fletcher, and I've forgotten who else called me
in the office.
I remember one, they had gotten an award, and I'd been
at the award meeting, and they called me in the office,
and they were saying, they said, well, Charlie,
why don't you just step down right now,
and we can start hunting, I mean, not right now,
but step down here in a month or two,
and then we can start looking for a new department head.
I said, well, I told you I was going to step down in August.
What's wrong with that?
Well, we could just do so much better if you just,
you know, step down, and I said, well, I don't care
to until the time allocated, and so they got Richardson
in there, and they went at it again.
I said, when Richardson, I said, if you want me to step
down so damn bad, I'll quit this minute.
Do you want it?
And that was the end of it.
They didn't want it that fast, and so I had, I served
on until exactly when I told them I'd,
you know, I'd be retired.
I said, well, I don't care.
I was ready to step down.
All right, let's go back to Mr. Rutter.
Mr. Rutter dies in early 1970.
Yeah, I had arranged for him to give a paper over in,
I believe it was Atlanta, and I had written it
on continuing education, and I went in to see him, and he,
I believe it was really dragging on him, because he said, Charlie,
I just don't see how I can do this.
I said, sure, you can do it.
All you got to do is read it.
I'll write the dang thing.
All you got to do is read it.
And Morgan was, used to be here as president, was the one
that was, had asked me to give him, get something,
get him to give a paper.
And so it turned out that Fred Benson had to read it.
He had the stroke before that occurred.
So I'd just been over to his office a little before that.
All right, then we briefly had Clyde Freeman, I believe it is,
as the interim, and then Jack Williams.
Let's see, no, we had Ludeke.
Oh, I'm sorry.
General Ludeke.
General Ludeke, yes, yes.
Did Ludeke have any, did you have any interaction with him?
Oh, yeah.
He asked me to look into this faculty club.
And I looked all over the place and found out that unless you had drinks,
if you wanted one to make profit, you're going to have to have something like that.
And another thing that I asked him to do, and it was already too late,
I said the way that thing is designed over there, you can't have a meeting in it.
And it needs to be to where you can have all the people in there
and have something in the faculty club that's a dinner of some sort where you have a speaker.
And that way, you can, it can make money that way.
And he said, well, it's too late to change it.
And he wanted me to, and I did, and I finally just reported to him that I didn't think,
that they could not make a go of it unless they had some, and they, it's, I've been proven
correct.
Right.
I was a junior member of that.
Oh.
Yeah.
I was a junior member of that committee for that, all of that expansion of the MSC and
the Rudder Tower.
And I was arguing the same thing as you because the faculty club on the 11th and 12th floors,
each of those little dining areas is so small that you can't get enough people in one place.
And so I told them that, and it wasn't.
But the architect is?
Somebody saw it, one in Dallas, like that, and I think I've eaten in that one in Dallas.
And that's the way they wanted it designed, that's the reason it got designed that way.
And Rudder, and Ludeke said, well, it's just too late, you know, to change it.
And he accepted it.
He just didn't like it much, but he, I just told him, you know, we just can't, it's hard
enough to get people to go out at night and eat anyway.
What would you, what description would you give of the manner in which he filled in as
the interim president?
Oh, I think he was, I think he was an excellent individual.
I think General Ludeke was an excellent individual.
I think General Ludeke was an excellent individual.
I think General Ludeke was one of the top people that, and actually Fred told me that
General Rudder got him, just hired him.
And Fred said, I tried to get him to take Ludeke and let him help him administer, and
he wouldn't do it.
He wanted to do it all himself.
And so he gave him to me.
But he ran the station, I worked for him some of them there.
But he was really a nice individual.
Describe his.
Very.
Very low key, but very definite.
I sat with him on committee meetings, and he didn't throw his former rank or anything
around.
And in fact, I was just over interviewing the transportation department.
His son is named Al Ludeke, and he's head of all planning over there.
And I didn't know about it until I called over there.
The same Al Ludeke, he went to A&M, but his son, Al Ludeke's son is also named Al Ludeke,
and he's running the planning division of the Texas Transportation Department, TxDOT.
And I went over and we had an interview with him.
The few times I was in his office, I never saw a more orderly, cleaner desk.
Everything seemed to be in order.
And with me, he asked very few questions.
Mm-hm.
He was always very skillfully planned, and he seemed to be an extremely good listener.
Mm-hm.
Well, you see, what happened was that after the war, the Atomic Energy Commission, they
put him in.
He was running that, but you never heard of him.
He wasn't out there making a lot of racket.
Then they had trouble with that space laboratory out in Stanford.
And so they sent him out there, and they had been screwing up.
When he got out there, they started working.
Yep.
And so everywhere he went, he was able to solve the problems, and he didn't make a big
stir about it.
So I like, he was a chemical engineering graduate, class of 1932.
Now he came back after serving as interim president, he came back to engineering.
Is that right?
Yeah, no.
He was serving in engineering before he ever became.
Well, but I mean, he went over to the president, then?
Well, I'm not really sure he ever really served any over there.
I think that was just a discussion before.
Oh, okay.
And then whenever, when Rutter died, they took him out of engineering and made him acting
president.
All right, but then after Williams came, he came back over to?
He may have.
I don't really remember.
Jack Williams came.
Describe your impressions of Jack Williams as president.
Well, I think he was the man for that time.
I'm not really sure that I.
You don't have a chance to say that you'd rank him anywhere.
Oh, I think he was one of the, I don't know, that I'd rank him anywhere near Rutter.
I think Rutter is one of the best ones that had ever been here, but yep, I would have
given him the poorest chances when he came, of anybody we've ever had.
But I think Jack did a good job as president, and because of his academic background, he
could see what was needed.
I think anything he did to get .
Then what did you think Jack did?
I think he did a slope.
I think he did a slope.
I think he did a slope.
I think he did a slope.
I think he did a slope.
